coredata.dc:title: Graphene nanomaterials supported palladium nanoparticles as nanocatalysts for electro-oxidation of methanol
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhongping
Ruan, Minna
Du, Liqing
Wen, Guangming
Dong, Chuan
Li, Hung-Wing
coredata.dc:description: We herein report a facile hydrothermal approach to prepare well-dispersed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-supported Pd (N-GQDs/Pd) nanocomposites of a narrow size distribution with diameter of 4.8±0.2nm. The N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite demonstrates an excellent electrocatalytic activity for the methanol oxidation reaction in an alkaline medium when compared with the graphene sheets-supported Pd nanocomposites (GS/Pd), graphene quantum dots supported Pd nanocomposites (GQDs/Pd) and commercial palladium catalyst (Pd/C). The N-GQDs/Pd of good dispersion and, small and narrow size distribution offers superior electrical conductivity and remarkable cycling stability. In addition, it offers promising and attractive practical opportunities for electronic materials which possess high catalytic activity and stability, yet the noble metal usage is reduced. This study demonstrated the potential use of N-GQDs/Pd as new electrocatalytic nanomaterials for direct methanol fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots(N-GQDs)
Palladium nanoparticles
Methanol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparative strategy for GS/Pd, GQDs/Pd and N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposites from OGS.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images, (a) GQDs/Pd nanoparticles in an average size of 9.8nm; (b) GS/Pd nanoparticles with average size of 12nm; (c) N-GQDs/Pd nanoparticles displayed a narrow size distribution with diameter ranging 3.8−6.2nm, the mean size is 4.8±0.2nm, from about 100 particles. (d) TGA cures of the (i) GS/Pd; (ii) GQDs/Pd; (iii) N-GQDs/Pd in the range of 30 to 800 °C at the heating rate of 5 °C· min−1 under nitrogen flow (20ml·min−1).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of (a) Pd 3d, (b) N 1s in GQDs (i), N-GQDs (ii) and N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite (iii), and (c) FTIR spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Pd (i: GO, ii: N-GQDs and; iii: N-GQDs/Pd).

(a) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of (i) N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode, (ii) GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode, (iii) bare Pd, (iv) GS/Pd and (v) Pd/C modified GC electrode in 1.0M NaOH at scan rate 0.05V/s; (b) CVs of 0.5M methanol oxidation in 1.0M NaOH on (i) N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode, (ii) GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode, (iii) bare Pd, (iv) GS/Pd and (v) Pd/C modified GC electrode at scan rate 0.05V/s.

(a) Stability of N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode in 1.0M NaOH with 0.1M methanol in 0.05Vs−1 by 100 potential cycles. (b) Chronoamperometric response cures to 0.5M methanol in 1.0M NaOH at −0.23V to (i) bare GC, (ii) Pd and (iii) N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrodes.
Summary of electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs) of the modified electrode and amounts of catalysts used.

head.ce:title: Graphene nanomaterials supported palladium nanoparticles as nanocatalysts for electro-oxidation of methanol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong SAR, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Z. Li, Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein report a facile hydrothermal approach to prepare well-dispersed nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-supported Pd (N-GQDs/Pd) nanocomposites of a narrow size distribution with diameter of 4.8±0.2nm. The N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite demonstrates an excellent electrocatalytic activity for the methanol oxidation reaction in an alkaline medium when compared with the graphene sheets-supported Pd nanocomposites (GS/Pd), graphene quantum dots supported Pd nanocomposites (GQDs/Pd) and commercial palladium catalyst (Pd/C). The N-GQDs/Pd of good dispersion and, small and narrow size distribution offers superior electrical conductivity and remarkable cycling stability. In addition, it offers promising and attractive practical opportunities for electronic materials which possess high catalytic activity and stability, yet the noble metal usage is reduced. This study demonstrated the potential use of N-GQDs/Pd as new electrocatalytic nanomaterials for direct methanol fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Palladium nanoparticles (Pd NPs) have been proven to be marvelous metallic heterogeneous catalysts for a wide range of chemical reactions. Preparation of palladium nanocrystals (Pd NCs) of well-defined size and shape is one of the most challenging goals in developing highly active Pd-based catalysts. The support is one of the most crucial factors because it can improve some of the desirable properties of the resulting catalyst, such as mechanical strength, stability, activity, and selectivity. Pd nanoparticles supported by carbon-based, including active carbon, carbon fibers, nanotubes, copolymer and graphene, are promising for a variety of applications ranging from catalysis, chemical and bio-sensing, to fuel cells and other renewable energy-related areas [1–5]. Effective control in morphology and size of Pd nanostructures allows the enhancement of their catalytic properties and activity on a mass or specific basis [6]. Despite significant work related to carbon-supported Pd nanoparticles have been reported, tailoring the properties of the support for catalysis remains a challenge.

Compared with conventional carbon supports, nitrogen-doped carbon materials can (i) increase the electric conductivity and surface area; (ii) enhance the affinity between the catalysts and supports through electrostatic and coordinative action; and (iii) avoid the migration and aggregation of nanocatalysts, and thus improve the durability of catalysts [7,8]. It is recognized that nitrogen doping offers active sites for deposition of metal particles on the support with better dispersion, smaller particle size, and more active surface area of electrocatalyst [9]. Higher doping amount of nitrogen favors a stronger adhesion of the Pd to the support [10]. The Pd nanoparticle-decorated nitrogen-doped grapheme (Pd/NG) catalyst exhibited excellent eletrocatalytic activity towards the ORR in alkaline media [11]. In contrast to two dimensional graphene nanosheets and one dimensional nanoribbons, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess strong quantum confinement and edge effects when their sizes are as small as ~10nm, at which unique physical properties are induced [12]. For the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), the chemically bonded N atoms could drastically produce new phenomena and unexpected properties, for example, altering the electronic characteristics and providing high number of anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions [13]. At the same time, N-GQDs possess many oxygen-containing functional groups that can make it more hydrophilic and also easier to form a stable chemical bond with other materials. Therefore, N-GQDs could provide high electrical conductivity which gives Pd nanoparticles supported on N-GQDs the superior auxiliary catalytic activity [14]. Herein, we employed a simple and direct approach to prepare novel spherical Pd nanoparticles supported on nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs/Pd). The covalent interaction between the Pd particles and N-GQDs was supported by infrared (IR) spectroscopy, which provides important insights regarding the possible mechanisms of carbon-supported Pd particles. The electrocatalytic activity of the as-prepared N-GQDs/Pd nanomaterials for methanol oxidation was investigated and compared with GS/Pd, GQDs/Pd and commercial catalyst Pd/C for oxidation of methanol in alkaline solution. We demonstrated that N-GQDs/Pd possesses a superior electrocatalytic performance and remarkable cycling stability.

(NH4)2PdCl4 the graphite powder; 5% Nafion methanol solution and Pd/C catalyst (containing 10wt% Pd); and Hydrazine hydrate solution 78–82% (acidimetric) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC (USA) and rod glassy carbon electrodes (3-mm, GC) were from Shanghai Chenghua Co. Ltd. (China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used upon purchase without further purification. The pure water for solution preparation was from a Millipore Autopure WR600A system (USA).

Morphologies of Pd NPs and other nanocomposites observed by TEM and HRTEM were performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy with an acceleration voltage of 200kV. All TEM samples were prepared by depositing a drop of diluted suspension in ethanol on a copper grid coated with carbon film. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku D/max-2500 using Cu Ka radiation. Electronic binding energies of C1s, N1s and Pd3d were measured by XPS analysis which was performed on a PHI Quantum 2000 Scanning ESCA Microprobe with a monochromatised microfocused Al X-ray source. All the binding energies were calibrated by using C1s as reference energy (C1s =284.6eV). Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Nicolet Magna 550 Series II FTIR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of sample was performed on a Pyris Diamond TG/DTA thermogravimetric analyzer (Perkin-Elmer Thermal Analysis). Sample was heated from 30 to 800°C at the rate of 5°Cmin−1 under nitrogen flow (20mL·min−1).

All electrochemical studies were conducted on a CH Instrument CV 600E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc. USA) using a standard three-electrode configuration. A Pt wire (1-mm diameter, Chenghua) was employed as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode was the reference electrode. The working electrode was the GS/Pd GQDs/PD Pd/C and N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposites modified GC electrode. The N2 were introduced into 0.50M H2SO4 and 1.0M NaOH solution at a flow rate of 100mL·min−1 for 15–30min. Electrochemical methods including cyclic voltammetry (CV), a scan rate of 0.05V·s−1 was used. Linear sweep voltammetry and chronoamperometry were used to study the electrochemical behaviors of bare GC, bare Pd and the N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrodes towards methanol oxidation in 1.0M NaOH at ambient conditions.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared by chemical oxidization of graphite powder according to the modified Hummers method as reported [15]. Graphene sheets (GSs) were obtained by reduction of GO sheets with NaBH4 at 85 °C for 12h. The graphene sheets (0.05g) were then oxidized in concentrated H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (30mL) for 15–20h under mild sonication (500W, 40kHz) [16]. The oxidized graphene sheets (OGSs) were diluted and purified with microporous membrane and then redispersed in deionized water. Homogeneous OGSs water dispersion (5mL) was mixed with 3.0mL of ammonia solution (28wt% in water) and 5mL of deionized water in a glass vial. After stirring for 30min, the mixture was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50mL) and heated at 180°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the insoluble precipitates were filtered out using a microporous membrane to obtain a brown filtrate. To remove large graphene nanoparticles, the colloidal solution was dialyzed in a dialysis kit (retained molecular weight 3500Da) for 5days. The N-GQDs was found to be stable for more than 3months.

(NH4)2PdCl4 weighing 28.4mg (0.10mM) was dissolved in 20mL of aqueous solution under vigorous stirring. An appropriate concentration, determined by the desired Pd2+/N-GQDs weight ratio, of 10mL of N-GQDs colloidal solution was added into the (NH4)2PdCl4 solution to obtain a dispersion mixture. After 12h of stirring, a 5.0mL aqueous solution of NaBH4 (37mg, 1.0mM) was added dropwisely and stirred until the mixture turned black, indicating the formation of Pd NPs. The black mixture was stirred for further 30min. The sample was isolated from the solvent by centrifugation at 13000rpm.

GQDs/Pd was obtained by the same procedure except that 3.0mL of ammonia solution (28wt% in water) was replaced with 3.0mL of 1.0M NaOH solution. GS/Pd was prepared by one step method by mixing the homogeneous OGSs water dispersion (5mL) with 10mL of the 0.1M (NH4)2PdCl4 solution. After 12h of stirring, a 10.0mL of 78–82% hydrazine hydrate solution was added dropwisely and stirred until the mixture turned black, indicating the formation of Pd NPs. The sample was isolated from the solvent by centrifugation at 13000rpm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was applied to investigate the structural composition of the as-prepared N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Pd. The characteristic peaks were compared in their corresponding XPS spectra (Fig. 3a and b). The core level binding energies (BEs) and the full-width at half maximum (fwhm) of the diffraction peaks were measured with particular attention to the Pd 3d spin-orbit components. The BEs of 335.0 and 340.6eV in the XPS of Pd3s in N-GQDs as displayed in Fig. 3a corresponded to the components of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of metallic Pd (0), respectively [20–24]. In addition, a pronounced but distinctly different BEs N 1s peak was observed for the resulted N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Pd (Fig. 3b). The N 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs evidenced the presence of both pyridine-like (397.8eV) and pyrrolic (399.4eV) N atoms [25,26]. The N 1s peak of N-GQDs/Pd (curve ii) however shows higher BEs compared to that of N-GQDs (curve i), inferring that the N element of the N-GQDs/Pd was relatively more electropositive than its free ammonia ligand. This suggested that some charge transfer from the N to surrounding Pd [27,28] which was also in good agreement with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra result as discussed below.

FTIR spectra of GO, N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Pd were displayed in Fig. 3c. The FTIR spectrum of GO displayed the presence of CO (νC–O at 1097cm−1), COH (νCOH at 1384cm−1), CO in carboxylic acid and carbonyl moieties (νCO at 1635 and 1726cm−1) and the H-bond of OH (broad band around 3410cm−1). Upon ammonia reduction of GO, the band at 1384, 1630 and 1726cm−1 almost disappeared while the band at 3410cm−1 was weakened significantly in the FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs. These indicated the dehydration and reduction of OH and C=O groups. In addition, the existence of new bands at 1492 and 1308cm−1 corresponding to NH in-plane and CN bands, respectively, confirmed the amine groups and elucidated the successful introduction of nitrogen element in the N-GQDs. It was noted that the NH stretch was heavily perturbed by metallic Pd in close proximity [29]. The band at 3440cm−1 corresponding to the NH stretching mode was weakened significantly in N-GQDs/Pd, and was distinctly different from that of the N-GQDs. The spectral peaks of N-GQDs were generally sharp, while those of N-GQDs/Pd were relatively broader and some of the peaks were almost unobservable.

Well-dispersed and ultrafine Pd NPs formed on graphene quantum dots are attractive for reducing the usage of noble metal. It was reported that graphene with zigzag edges offers extraordinary electronic or magnetic properties [3]. To evaluate its catalytic performance, we compared the CVs obtained with N-GQDs/Pd modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode with those of GQDs/Pd, GS/Pd and Pd/C modified GC electrodes in 0.5M H2SO4 at 0.05V·s−1 as depicted in Fig. S2. We summarized electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs) of the Modified Electrode and amounts of catalysts used in Table 1 . The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) of N-GQDs/Pd, GQDs/Pd, GS/Pd and Pd/C were estimated by the hydrogen desorption area determined from CV curves. The oxidation currents were normalized to the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of 47.6m2·g−1. Enhancement of 3.1-fold, 3.2-fold, 6.0-fold were achieved with N-GQDs/Pd when compared to that of GQDs/Pd, GS/Pd and commercial Pd/C catalyst. N-GQDs/Pd exhibited superior catalytic performance in contrast to GQDs/Pd, GS/Pd nanocomposites although N-GQDs/Pd only consists of one quarter Pd content of those GQDs/Pd and GS/Pd. The optimal amount of applied N-GQDs/Pd was determined to be 10μL (1μg) per electrode (Fig. S3).

The nanocomposite of N-GQDs/Pd was evaluated for its performance towards an electrocatalytic oxidation of methanol as a demonstration. GC electrodes modified with the as-prepared (i) N-GQDs/Pd, (ii) GQDs/Pd, (iv) GS/Pd and (v) Pd/C; and (iii) bare Pd electrode was used for CV measurement of in 1.0M NaOH with and without 0.5M methanol. The peak current density with N-GQDs/Pd was found to be ∼2.5, ∼3, ∼4.5 and ∼5 times of that on GQD/Pd, bare Pd, GS/Pd and commercial Pd/C catalyst in 1.0M NaOH without 0.5M methanol (Fig. 4a). N-GQDs/Pd showed superior catalytic activity in alkaline media among these five electrodes. Similarly, N-GQDs/Pd demonstrated the most superior electrocatalytic oxidation activity for methanol (Fig. 4b). In the presence of 0.5M methanol, the onset of methanol oxidation on N-GQDs/Pd was earlier than that on GQDs/Pd bare Pd GS/Pd and Pd/C. The peak current intensity generated with N-GQDs/Pd was ∼11-fold and 16-fold respectively higher than that with commercial Pd/C catalyst in the positive- and negative going potential scans. The excellent catalytic activity was mainly attributed to better dispersion and smaller size of Pd nanoparticles on N-GQDs and, probably higher adsorption of methanol on N-GQDs/Pd catalyst as well. Among these five electrode materials, the N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode produced a pronounced oxidation peak in the CV curve at around 0.0V (vs Ag/AgCl) in the forward sweep and a sharp reduction peak at −0.32V in 1.0M NaOH solution, indicating that the N-GQDs/Pd modified GC electrode is very stable. These results supported that N-GQDs/Pd possesses superior catalytic activity than GQDs/Pd, bare Pd, GS/Pd and Pd/C towards methanol oxidation. For the reusability and stability of the N-GQDs/Pd modified electrode, a slight drop of 5.4% current peak (methanol oxidation) of the initial catalytic activity in the forward scan was found after 100cycles were performed. The durability of it was much higher than those of the bare Pd electrode as depicted in Fig. 5a. Lastly, we compared the chronoamperometric response towards methanol at −0.23V among GC electrode, bare Pd electrode and Pd@N-GQDs modified GC electrode. As shown in Fig. 5b, a significantly higher (11-fold enhancement) current response was provided by Pd@N-GQDs modified GC electrode elucidating its superior electrocatalytic activity. In short, these results supported that N-GQDs/Pd modified electrode is much more electro-active and stable compared to bare Pd electrode in both acidic and alkaline media. The high electrocatalytic activity and stability of the N-GQDs/Pd may arise from any (or all) of the following effects: (i) N-GQDs has a large surface area and is capable of adsorbing more CH3OH, facilitating its activation by the adjacent Pd NPs; (ii) N-GQDs is highly conductive and can promote electron transfer across the GC-electrode and Pd NPs interfaces; [30] (iii) the strong Pd NPs interactions with N-GQDs, efficiently stabilizes Pd NPs against the undesired corrosion as observed in the C-Pt catalyst [31]. Thus, the novelty of the current work relies on the exploration of the roles of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots.

We developed a direct approach to prepare well-dispersed, highly stable and electrocatalytically active and stable N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite from OGS by simple hydrothermal treatment in the presence of ammonia. No further surface modification is required. The N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite exhibited an excellent electrocatalytic activity when compared to GS/Pd, GQD/Pd, and commercial Pd/C catalyst. The N-GQDs/Pd nanocomposite offers great potential for a broad range of applications, including biosensors, chemical synthesis and electrochemical catalytic oxidation. It could serve as a new approach to reduction in usage of noble metal but highly efficient catalyst design for bioscience applications, and energy technology, such as oxidation-reduction in fuel cells. These results have important implications on electrocatalytic oxidation reactions and design of electronics made of carbon nanotubes or graphene where metallic contact is necessary.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575083 and 21205075), Shanxi “Hundred Talent Program” (2015 H.W. Li), and research project supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2013-027 and 2016-8). We also thank the financial support of the Hong Kong Baptist University (FRG2/14-15/057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.10.015.
